1: A shadow: Ps 6 is the first of the seven penitential psalms (6, 32, 38, 51, 102, 130, 143). It opens with the cry not to be rebuked in divine anger — the fear of divine wrath that the covenant people rightly felt under the law's condemnation. Christ absorbs this rebuking: 'Christ redeemed us from the curse of the law by becoming a curse for us' (Galatians 3:13). The rebuking anger that Ps 6 fears is the anger Christ bore, so that those who trust him receive instead the blessing.
2: A shadow: 'be gracious to me, O LORD, for I am languishing; heal me, O LORD, for my bones are troubled.' The embodied suffering of the penitent — bones troubled, soul terrified. Christ entered this embodied vulnerability fully: in Gethsemane, 'his sweat became like great drops of blood falling down to the ground' (Luke 22:44); on the cross, the Johannine account notes that not a bone was broken (John 19:36, fulfilling Psalms 34:20) — yet he bore the full spiritual anguish of the troubled soul.
3: A shadow: 'my soul also is greatly troubled. But you, O LORD — how long?' The 'how long?' of the penitent's cry — the central question of lament. At Golgotha, 'My God, my God, why have you forsaken me?' (Psalms 22:1) is the ultimate 'how long?' The silence of God that the penitent experiences is what Christ entered to its absolute depth, so that the believer who prays 'how long?' does so as one who knows that YHWH has already answered in the resurrection.
4: A shadow: 'turn, O LORD, deliver my soul; save me for the sake of your steadfast love.' The petition grounded in chesed — save not because of my merit but because of your covenant love. This is the ground of all atonement: YHWH saves 'for the sake of your steadfast love' (lema'an chasdecha). Christ's atoning work is itself the supreme expression of divine chesed (John 3:16: 'for God so loved the world that he gave...') — the covenant-love given its fullest form in the cross.
5: A shadow: 'for in death there is no remembrance of you; in Sheol who will give you praise?' The OT concern that death silences praise. Christ's descent into death (1 Peter 3:18-19; Ephesians 4:9) and his resurrection out of it is the answer to this fear: he went where praise was impossible and returned, making the resurrection the foundation of eternal praise. Hebrews 2:12 quotes Psalms 22:22 ('I will tell of your name to my brothers; in the midst of the congregation I will sing your praise') as what the risen Christ does — praise has been restored from the dead.
6: A shadow: 'I am weary with my moaning; every night I flood my bed with tears; I drench my couch with my weeping.' The depths of the penitent's grief — the physical expression of spiritual distress. Jesus wept (John 11:35) — the Son of God entering the full range of human grief. Hebrews 5:7 describes Jesus in Gethsemane: 'he offered up prayers and supplications, with loud cries and tears, to him who was able to save him from death.' The weeping of Ps 6 finds its fullest expression and its final answer in Christ's tears and resurrection.
7: A shadow: 'my eye wastes away because of grief; it grows weak because of all my foes.' The physical dimming through grief — the body bearing the weight of spiritual distress. The 'foes' who cause this grief are, in Christ's case, the systemic powers of sin, death, and the devil that he entered combat with. His 'weakness' at the cross (2 Corinthians 13:4: 'he was crucified in weakness') is the entry point for divine power: 'he was raised by the power of God.'
8: A theme: 'depart from me, all you workers of evil, for the LORD has heard the sound of my weeping.' The confident reversal — the psalmist's assurance that YHWH has heard. Jesus uses the language of this verse ('depart from me') in Matthew 7:23, addressed to those who called him Lord but did not know him, and in Matthew 25:41, addressed to those who failed to serve him in the poor. The 'depart from me' of Psalms 6:8 becomes the eschatological judgment-word of the Son.
9: A theme: 'the LORD has heard my plea; the LORD accepts my prayer.' The confident assurance of answered prayer — what the penitent gains from entering the suffering of Ps 6. Christ's prayers were always heard by the Father (John 11:42). And through Christ, the prayers of those who come to God 'through him' are accepted (Hebrews 7:25: 'he is able to save to the uttermost those who draw near to God through him, since he always lives to make intercession for them').
10: A theme: 'all my enemies shall be ashamed and greatly troubled; they shall turn back and be put to shame in a moment.' The final reversal — the shame that the penitent feared (v1: fear of divine rebuke) is redirected to the enemies. The resurrection is the moment when the shame is redirected: those who killed the Son are confronted with the one they crucified (Acts 2:36: 'let all the house of Israel therefore know for certain that God has made him both Lord and Christ, this Jesus whom you crucified'). The 'in a moment' of the reversal anticipates the sudden transformation of the third day.
